title: fCUT&Tag‐Seq: An optimized CUT&Tag‐based method for high‐resolution profiling of histone modifications and chromatin‐binding proteins in fungi
author: Wang, Haiting
materials: Primary antibodies specific to the target histone modifications are introduced, followed by secondary antibodies that serve to amplify the detection signal. (

ChIP assays were performed as previously described
56
, 
57
, 
58
. Mycelia were cross‐linked with a 1% formaldehyde solution (0.4 M sucrose, 10 mM Tris‐HCl [pH 8.0], 1 mM EDTA, 1 mM PMSF, 1% formaldehyde) at room temperature for 15 min, with the flask inverted every 2–3 min. To stop cross‐linking, glycine was added to a final concentration of 0.2 M, followed by incubation for 5 min. Fixed cells were then washed with 1× PBS, cryo‐milled in liquid nitrogen, and lysed in lysis buffer (50 mM HEPES [pH 7.5], 137 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 0.1% SDS, 1% Triton X‐100, 0.1% sodium deoxycholate, 1 mM PMSF, 1 μg/ml leupeptin, 1 μg/ml pepstatin A). Chromatin was sonicated to an average DNA fragment size of ~500–1000 bp. After sonication, samples were centrifuged at 10,000g and 4°C for 15 min to collect the supernatant. For immunoprecipitation, 2 μl of the anti‐H3K9ac antibody (Cat. No. 39161; Active Motif) was incubated with aliquots of clarified cell lysates (containing 2 mg of protein each) at 4°C overnight with rotation. After overnight incubation, 50 μl of SS DNA/Protein G Sepharose (pretreated three times with lysis buffer) was added, followed by further incubation at 4°C for 2 h. Sepharose beads were collected and washed once with 1 ml of each of the following buffers: high‐salt wash buffer (lysis buffer supplemented with 0.5 M NaCl), 1× LNDET (0.25 M LiCl, 1% NP40, 1% deoxycholate, 1 mM EDTA, 10 mM Tris [pH 8]), and 50 mM Tris‐HCl [pH 8], and 1 mM EDTA. Immune complexes were eluted from the beads using freshly prepared elution buffer (1% SDS, 0.1 M NaHCO₃) via two rounds of elution at room temperature (250 μl per elution).

steps.title: We developed an optimized fCUT&Tag‐Seq specifically...
steps.instruction: We developed an optimized fCUT&Tag‐Seq specifically adapted for fungal systems, building upon the traditional approach using the Vazyme Hyperactive Universal CUT&Tag Assay Kit for Illumina Pro (Cat. TD904). Our optimization addresses the unique challenges posed by fungal cell architecture while maintaining the high sensitivity and specificity of the CUT&Tag‐Seq method. The modified protocol incorporates several key steps for fungal applications (Figure 1). To overcome the barrier presented by the fungal cell wall, we added a controlled enzymatic digestion step to generate protoplasts, followed by gentle nuclear extraction to preserve chromatin integrity. The isolated nuclei are then specifically captured by Concanavalin Concanavalin A (ConA) coated magnetic beads, which provide a stable platform for subsequent reactions while minimizing sample loss.

steps.title: Nuclear membrane permeabilization is enhanced through...
steps.instruction: Nuclear membrane permeabilization is enhanced through carefully optimized digitonin treatment, facilitating antibody access while maintaining nuclear structural integrity. (

steps.title: The pA/G‐Tn5 transposase complex is recruited...
steps.instruction: The pA/G‐Tn5 transposase complex is recruited to the antibody‐bound locations through protein A/G interactions. (

steps.title: Upon Mg2+ activation, the tethered Tn5...
steps.instruction: Upon Mg2+ activation, the tethered Tn5 simultaneously cleaves adjacent DNA and integrates sequencing adapters, enabling efficient library preparation through subsequent PCR amplification. After library construction, next‐generation sequencing and data analysis can be performed to map the distribution pattern of histone modifications.

steps.title: Genome‐wide profiling revealed robust enrichment of...
steps.instruction: Genome‐wide profiling revealed robust enrichment of both H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 modifications in wild‐type strains (Figure 3A,B). H3K9me3 is a repressive mark mainly localized in constitutive heterochromatic regions, while H3K27me3 is a repressive mark responsible for facultative gene silencing, usually localized in euchromatin. In contrast, control experiments using nonspecific IgG antibodies showed minimal signal across the genome, confirming the specificity of our approach (Figure S3A). Importantly, we observed significantly reduced levels of H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 in their respective methyltransferase mutants, VdΔkmt1 and VdΔezh2, consistent with the loss of these enzymatic activities (Figure 3A,B).

steps.title: Genome‐wide profiling of histone modifications in V
steps.instruction: Genome‐wide profiling of histone modifications in V. dahliae using fCUT&Tag‐Seq. (A) Genome browser view of H3K9me3 distribution in wild‐type and VdΔkmt1 strains. (B) Genome browser view of H3K27me3 distribution in wild‐type and VdΔezh2 strains. (C) Comparative analysis of genomic feature distribution for H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 modifications between fCUT&Tag‐Seq and ChIP‐Seq approaches. The left panel shows the distribution of H3K9me3 peaks across different genomic elements and the right panel shows H3K27me3 peak distribution. (D) Pearson correlation analysis of signals between biological replicates for H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 modifications in ChIP‐Seq (replicate 1 and replicate 2) and fCUT&Tag‐Seq experiments (replicate 1 and replicate 2). Pearson correlations were determined using deepTools, with the multiBamSummary tool, followed by plotCorrelation. Read counts were divided into 1000‐bp bins across the genome for the analysis.

steps.title: Analysis of the genomic distribution of...
steps.instruction: Analysis of the genomic distribution of these modifications revealed distinct patterns. Genomic feature distribution showed that H3K9me3 peaks were predominantly located in gene promoter regions and distal intergenic spaces in constitutive heterochromatin, while H3K27me3 was mostly located in the gene promoter region in euchromatin (Figure 3C). These distribution patterns align with the known roles of these modifications in transcriptional regulation. A comparison of signal intensities between fCUT&Tag‐Seq and ChIP‐Seq data demonstrated superior enrichment in fCUT&Tag‐Seq experiments, particularly across gene bodies and their 3‐kb flanking regions (Figure S3B).

steps.title: To assess the reproducibility of our...
steps.instruction: To assess the reproducibility of our modified method, we performed correlation analyses between biological replicates. fCUT&Tag‐Seq demonstrated excellent reproducibility, with Pearson correlation coefficients of 0.99 and 1.00 for H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 modifications, respectively. These values exceeded those obtained from traditional ChIP‐Seq experiments (0.91 and 0.89), highlighting the enhanced reliability of our optimized method (Figure 3D).

steps.title: These results collectively demonstrate that our...
steps.instruction: These results collectively demonstrate that our fCUT&Tag‐Seq protocol enables highly specific and reproducible mapping of histone modifications in V. dahliae, providing a robust tool for investigating chromatin‐based regulation in this important plant pathogen.

steps.title: To assess whether our fCUT&Tag‐Seq protocol...
steps.instruction: To assess whether our fCUT&Tag‐Seq protocol could be extended beyond V. dahliae, we tested it on two additional filamentous fungi: N. crassa and Fusarium graminearum. Both species serve as valuable model organisms for studying fungal biology and pathogenicity, making them ideal candidates for validating broad applicability. Following the same experimental workflow, we focused on histone modifications H3K9me3 in N. crassa and H3K4me3 in F. graminearum.

steps.title: N
steps.instruction:
N. crassa, a member of the Ascomycota phylum, is widely recognized as a model organism due to its ease of cultivation and haploid genetics, which facilitate straightforward genetic analysis. Over the past few decades, it has been instrumental in studying various epigenetic modifications, including DNA methylation and gene silencing mechanisms
26
, 
27
, 
28
. We examined the levels of H3K9me3 in both the wild‐type strain and the H3K9me3 methyltransferase mutant NcΔdim5. Quality control assessments, including agarose gel electrophoresis and sequencing data analysis, confirmed that the fCUT&Tag‐Seq libraries met the required standards (Figure 4A,B). The quality metrics indicated excellent performance for the N. crassa wild‐type samples (Figure 4C). Notably, the peak distribution patterns observed were consistent with those identified through ChIP‐Seq, yet fCUT&Tag‐Seq demonstrated higher signal intensity (Figure 4D). The correlation between fCUT&Tag‐Seq and ChIP‐Seq data reached 0.73 (Figure 4E), further validating the reliability of our method. Additionally, biological replicates showed a strong correlation, indicating high reproducibility (Figure 4F). Comparative analysis revealed that H3K9me3 was significantly enriched in the wild‐type strain, while the NcΔdim5 mutant displayed markedly lower modification levels (Figure 4G). As expected, H3K9me3 modifications were predominantly located in distal intergenic regions and promoter regions (Figure 4H).

steps.title: Application of fCUT&Tag‐Seq for histone modifications analysis in Neurospora crassa
steps.instruction: Application of fCUT&Tag‐Seq for histone modifications analysis in Neurospora crassa. (A) Nucleosomal ladder pattern analysis of fCUT&Tag‐Seq libraries by agarose gel electrophoresis. Libraries were generated from two replicates of H3K9me3 profiling experiments in N. crassa. (B) Fragment length distribution analysis of sequencing reads from fCUT&Tag‐Seq experiments. Histograms display the expected nucleosomal fragmentation patterns for two replicates. (C) Comparative analysis between fCUT&Tag‐Seq and ChIP‐Seq data for H3K9me3 modification in N. crassa. (D) Genome browser view of peak distribution comparison of fCUT&Tag‐Seq and ChIP‐Seq results. The upper panel shows H3K9me3 enrichment in ChIP‐Seq and the lower panel displays the modification distribution in fCUT&Tag‐Seq. (E) Correlation matrix analysis between fCUT&Tag‐Seq and ChIP‐Seq data, indicating strong agreement. Pearson correlations were determined using deepTools (multiBamSummary, followed by plotCorrelation), based on read counts divided into 1000‐bp bins across the genome. (F) Pearson correlation analysis of H3K9me3 signals between biological replicates in ChIP‐Seq and fCUT&Tag‐Seq. (G) Genome browser view of the H3K9me3 peaks across representative genomic regions in wild‐type and NcΔdim5 strains in N. crassa. (H) Feature distribution comparison of H3K9me3 modification between ChIP‐Seq and fCUT&Tag‐Seq.

steps.title: Similar results were obtained for F
steps.instruction:
Similar results were obtained for F. graminearum, the causative agent of Fusarium head blight in wheat
29
. In addition to analyzing H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 modifications, we also assessed H3K4me3 levels, which are associated with gene activation. The sequencing quality metrics of fCUT&Tag‐Seq for F. graminearum were superior to those of ChIP‐Seq, with a greater percentage of reads mapping to peak regions (Figure 5A,B). The SSD score and FRiP reached 22.5 and 46.5, respectively (Figure 5B). These findings confirm the robustness of our method, as the wild‐type strain showed significant enrichment (Figure 5C). Notably, peak enrichment was predominantly observed in the promoter regions (96.51%) (Figure 5D). The Pearson correlation coefficient was 0.99, indicating a strong correlation between the sample groups (Figure 5E).

steps.title: Genome‐wide profiling of H3K4me3 modification in...
steps.instruction: Genome‐wide profiling of H3K4me3 modification in Fusarium graminearum using fCUT&Tag‐Seq analysis. (A) Fragment length distribution of sequencing libraries for fCUT&Tag‐Seq. The left panel shows the fragment length distribution for the H3K4me3 library in replicate 1 and the right panel shows the distribution for replicate 2. (B) Comparative analysis of key quality metrics for ChIP‐Seq and fCUT&Tag‐Seq datasets. (C) Genome browser view of H3K4me3 modification peak distribution comparison of fCUT&Tag‐Seq and ChIP‐Seq results. The genome regions were randomly selected. Green peaks represent ChIP‐Seq results and blue peaks represent fCUT&Tag‐Seq results. (D) The pie chart and UpSet plot illustrating the distribution of peaks across different genomic features. H3K4me3 is predominantly enriched in promoter regions. Distinct colors represent different genomic features. (E) Pearson correlation coefficients between sample groups for ChIP‐Seq and fCUT&Tag‐Seq. The scatter plot displays normalized read counts in 1000‐bp bins across the genome.

steps.title: Overall, these results demonstrate that our...
steps.instruction: Overall, these results demonstrate that our fCUT&Tag‐Seq method is not only effective for detecting histone modifications in V. dahliae but also applicable to other model fungi, including N. crassa and F. graminearum. This versatility underscores the potential of fCUT&Tag‐Seq as a powerful tool for studying epigenetic modifications across a broad range of fungal species.

steps.title: To extend the utility of our...
steps.instruction: To extend the utility of our optimized fCUT&Tag‐Seq protocol beyond histone modifications, we adapted the method to profile chromatin‐binding proteins, which play crucial roles in transcriptional regulation. Given the typically lower abundance of chromatin‐binding proteins compared to histones, we incorporated an additional formaldehyde crosslinking step before protoplast preparation. This modification strengthens protein–chromatin interactions, thereby improving detection sensitivity. Following fragmentation, we performed sequential decrosslinking, protease digestion, and RNase treatment to ensure efficient DNA purification and library preparation (Figure 6).

steps.title: To validate this adapted fCUT&Tag‐Seq method,...
steps.instruction:
To validate this adapted fCUT&Tag‐Seq method, we investigated the histone acetyltransferase SsGcn5 in Sporisorium scitamineum, a dimorphic pathogenic fungus responsible for sugarcane smut. Sugarcane smut is one of the most significant diseases affecting global sugarcane production. The disease poses substantial economic threats, highlighting the urgent need for effective prevention and control strategies against S. scitamineum

30
, 
31
, 
32
. It has been reported that histone acetylation may play a critical role in fungal pathogenesis
33
, 
34
, 
35
, 
36
. To explore the relationship between histone acetylation and fungal pathogenicity, we screened possible histone acetyltransferase candidate genes and introduced a Flag epitope tag into the histone acetyltransferase Gcn5 in two S. scitamineum strains with different mating types (JG35 and JG36), using our previously reported method
37
.

steps.title: Quality control analysis of the resulting...
steps.instruction: Quality control analysis of the resulting fCUT&Tag‐Seq libraries revealed proper DNA fragmentation patterns by agarose gel electrophoresis and expected nucleosomal size distributions in sequencing reads (Figure 7A,B), confirming successful library preparation. Comprehensive quality metrics and genome‐wide profiling demonstrated robust enrichment of SsGcn5‐3Flag binding sites across the S. scitamineum genome (Figure 7C,D). Notably, comparative analysis revealed a strong correlation between SsGcn5 binding sites and regions of H3K18 acetylation (Figure 7E), consistent with the known catalytic activity of Gcn5. Feature distribution analysis showed that SsGcn5 binding sites were predominantly located in promoter regions (85.93% of total peaks) (Figure 7F), suggesting a primary role in transcriptional regulation. The high reproducibility of our method was demonstrated by the near‐perfect Pearson correlation coefficient (approximately 1.0) between biological replicates (Figure 7G).

steps.title: Genome‐wide profiling of SsGcn5 and its...
steps.instruction: Genome‐wide profiling of SsGcn5 and its correlation with H3K18ac modification in Sporisorium scitamineum using fCUT&Tag‐Seq. (A) Quality assessment of fCUT&Tag‐Seq libraries by agarose gel electrophoresis. DNA fragment distributions are shown for two biological replicates of JG35 (lanes 1–2) and JG36 (lanes 3–4) mating types. (B) Fragment length distribution analysis of sequencing reads demonstrating expected nucleosomal patterns. Blue and pink histograms represent Gcn5‐Flag distributions in JG35 and JG36 strains, respectively. (C) Comprehensive quality metrics for fCUT&Tag‐Seq datasets in DNA‐binding protein enrichment in JG35 and JG36 strains. (D) Genome browser view of SsGcn5‐Flag binding sites identified by fCUT&Tag‐Seq. Genome regions were selected randomly. (E) Correlation analysis between SsGcn5‐Flag binding sites and H3K18ac modification regions, showing the functional relationship between histone acetyltransferase localization and its catalytic activity. (F) Genomic feature distribution analysis of SsGcn5‐Flag peaks in JG35 and JG36 strains, showing the relative enrichment across different genomic elements (promoters, gene bodies, intergenic regions). (G) Correlation analysis between biological replicates for SsGcn5‐Flag fCUT&Tag‐Seq experiments.

steps.title: Our results confirmed that the fCUT&Tag‐Seq...
steps.instruction: Our results confirmed that the fCUT&Tag‐Seq method not only excels in detecting histone modifications but also effectively profiles chromatin‐binding proteins in fungi. By enhancing the detection of low‐abundance proteins, this method also provides a powerful tool for exploring chromatin‐binding proteins in regulating gene expression and chromatin dynamics across a broad range of fungal species.

steps.title: The following fungal strains were used in this study
steps.instruction:
The following fungal strains were used in this study: V. dahliae strain V592 (isolated from cotton in Xinjiang, China)
50
, 
51
, N. crassa 87‐3 and NcΔdim5 (provided by Xiao Liu)
52
, S. scitamineum strains JG35 and JG36 (provided by Shan Lu)
53
, and F. graminearum wild‐type strain (provided by Guangfei Tang and Haoxue Xia)
54
. All strains were maintained and cultured according to standard protocols specific to each species.

steps.title: were constructed as previously reported
steps.instruction:
were constructed as previously reported
55
. 1 kb genomic sequences upstream and downstream of target genes were amplified from V592 DNA with the following primer pairs: VdKMT1‐A/VdKMT1‐a, VdKMT1‐d/VdKMT1‐B, VdEZH2‐A/VdEZH2‐a, and VdEZH2‐d/VdEZH2‐B (listed in Table S

steps.title: using the Phanta Max Super‐Fidelity DNA...
steps.instruction: using the Phanta Max Super‐Fidelity DNA Polymerase (P505, Vazyme) and cloned into the pGKO‐HPT/pGKO‐NAT vector via homologous recombination. Sequence‐verified vectors were then used to transform V. dahliae by an Agrobacterium tumefaciens‐mediated transformation (ATMT) system. V. dahliae transformants were selected on potato dextrose agar (PDA) plates supplemented with 5‐fluoro‐2′deoxyuridine (5FU) and hygromycin B/Nourseothricin. Putative transformants were screened using PCR to confirm the successful deletion of the target genes with primer pairs V1/Hpt‐R and V2/Hpt‐F. The primers used for construction are listed in Table S1.

steps.title: For S
steps.instruction: For S. scitamineum, the SsGcn5‐Flag strain was constructed using homologous recombination. The vector pEX1‐GAP‐Flag‐HPT, carrying a Flag tag, was modified from pEX1‐GAP‐HPT. Approximately 1.5 kb of upstream (5′) and downstream (3′) flanking sequences near the target protein's stop codon (excluding the stop codon) were amplified from the wild‐type strains JG35 and JG36 using the primer pairs SsGcn5‐tg‐A/SsGcn5‐a and SsGcn5‐d/SsGcn5‐B, respectively (Table S1). Two truncated fragments of the hygromycin phosphotransferase gene HPT (HPT‐up and HPT‐down) were amplified from the pEX1‐GAP‐Flag‐HPT plasmid with the primer pairs HPT‐LB‐F/HPT‐LB‐R and HPT‐RB‐F/HPT‐RB‐R. Overlap PCR was used to fuse the upstream sequence with HPT‐up (primers SsGcn5‐tg‐A/HPT‐LB‐R) and the downstream sequence with HPT‐down (primers HPT‐RB‐F/SsGcn5‐B). The products were introduced into S. scitamineum strains JG35 and JG36 via PEG‐mediated protoplast transformation. Transformants were selected on YEPSA medium containing 150 μg/ml hygromycin B and verified by PCR using the primer pairs SsGcn5‐C(V1)/HPT‐LB‐R and SsGcn5‐D(V2)/HPT‐RB‐F.

steps.title: To profile histone modifications using fCUT&Tag‐Seq,...
steps.instruction: To profile histone modifications using fCUT&Tag‐Seq, protoplasts were prepared by digesting mycelium (V. dahliae, N. crassa, and F. graminearum) or spores (S. scitamineum) with 10 ml of enzyme solution (0.25 g of VinoTaste®Pro enzyme in 12.5 ml of 0.7 M NaCl for V. dahliae, N. crassa, and S. scitamineum; 0.1 g of driselase, 0.2 g of celulase, and 0.2 g of lysozyme were diluted with 0.7 M NaCl to 20 ml for F. graminearum). F. graminearum was incubated at 30°C with shaking at 85 rpm, while the others were incubated at 33°C at 60 rpm. Digestion times were 3 h for V. dahliae mycelium, F. graminearum mycelium, and N. crassa mycelium, and 30 min for S. scitamineum spores. Protoplasts were filtered through Miracloth (475855‐1 R; Merck) and centrifuged for 10 min at 3800 rpm (V. dahliae and N. crassa), 2200 rpm (S. scitamineum), or 5000 rpm (F. graminearum) at 25°C. Protoplasts were resuspended in 10 ml of 0.7 M NaCl and then counted using a hemocytometer.

steps.title: Nuclei were extracted from 1 million protoplasts per 1
steps.instruction: Nuclei were extracted from 1 million protoplasts per 1.5 ml tube by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min at 25°C, followed by resuspension in 100 μl of nuclear extract buffer (TD904; Vazyme), incubation on ice for 10 min, and a second centrifugation step at 2500 rpm for 6 min at 25°C. The supernatant was discarded, and nuclei were resuspended in 100 µl of wash buffer (TD904; Vazyme).

steps.title: Nuclei were adsorbed by mixing activated...
steps.instruction: Nuclei were adsorbed by mixing activated Concanavalin A‐coated magnetic beads (TD904; Vazyme) with 250,000 resuspended nuclei and 75 µl of wash buffer (TD904; Vazyme) in 200 μl 8‐row tubes, followed by incubation at room temperature for 10 min with 2–3 inversions.

steps.title: For antibody binding, tubes were placed...
steps.instruction: For antibody binding, tubes were placed on a magnetic rack, the supernatant was discarded, and 50 μl of antibody buffer (TD904; Vazyme) was added to resuspend the bead–nucleus complex. Primary antibodies (1 μl each) against H3K9me3 (Cat39161; Active Motif), H3K27me3 (Cat39155; Active Motif), or H3K4me3 (Cat39060; Active Motif) were added according to the ratio used. After mixing by inverting the tube, centrifugation was performed immediately, followed by refrigeration at 4°C for overnight incubation under static conditions (no rotation).

steps.title: The next day, the beads were...
steps.instruction: The next day, the beads were centrifuged briefly, placed on a magnetic rack, and the supernatant was discarded. 0.5 µl of secondary rabbit antibody (Ab207; Vazyme) in 49.5 µl of Dig wash buffer (792 μl Wash Buffer, 8 μl 5% Digitonin) was added and incubated with rotation (9–11 rpm/min) at room temperature for 1 h. Complexes were washed three times with 200 µl of Dig wash buffer.

steps.title: For transposase incubation, 2 µl of...
steps.instruction: For transposase incubation, 2 µl of Hyperactive pA/G‐Transposon (TD904; Vazyme) in 98 µl of Dig‐300 buffer (100 μl 10× Dig‐300 Buffer, 2 μl 5% Digitonin, 20 μl 50× protease inhibitor, 878 μl ddH2O) were added to each reaction and incubated with gentle rotation (9–11 rpm/min) at room temperature for 1 h. After instantaneous centrifugation and placing on a magnetic rack, the supernatant was discarded and washed three times using 200 µl of Dig‐300 buffer.

steps.title: For fragmentation, 10 µl of TruePrep...
steps.instruction: For fragmentation, 10 µl of TruePrep Tagment Buffer L (TD904; Vazyme) in 40 µl of Dig‐300 buffer was added to each reaction and incubated at 37°C for 1 h.

steps.title: DNA was extracted by adding 2...
steps.instruction: DNA was extracted by adding 2 µl of 10% SDS (TD904; Vazyme) and 0.5 µl of DNA spike‐in (TD904; Vazyme), incubating at 55°C for 10 min, transferring the supernatant to a new tube, adding 50 μl of activated DNA extract beads (TD904; Vazyme), incubating for 20 min at room temperature with mixing, washing twice with 200 μl of 1× B&W buffer (TD904; Vazyme), drying, and resuspending in 30 µl of RNAase‐free ddH2O.

steps.title: Libraries were constructed by PCR (50 μl reaction
steps.instruction: Libraries were constructed by PCR (50 μl reaction: 15 μl of bead‐bound DNA, 25 μl of 2× CUT&Tag Amplification Mix [TD904; Vazyme], 5 μl of N5XX Primer [TD202; Vazyme], 5 μl of N7XX Primer [TD202; Vazyme]) following Illumina's adaptor pooling guide. PCR conditions were as follows: 72°C for 3 min, 95°C for 3 min; then 9–20 cycles of 98°C for 10 s, 60°C for 5 s, 72°C for 1 min; and hold at 4°C.

steps.title: PCR products were purified by adding...
steps.instruction: PCR products were purified by adding 100 μl of VAHTS DNA clean beads (N411; Vazyme), incubating for 5 min, washing twice with 200 μl of 80% ethanol, drying, resuspending in 22 μl of RNase‐free ddH₂O, and incubating for 5 min before quality control and sequencing.

steps.title: To profile lower‐abundance chromatin‐binding proteins, a...
steps.instruction: To profile lower‐abundance chromatin‐binding proteins, a formaldehyde crosslinking step was added. Approximately 1 g of fungal spores was fixed with 9 ml of 0.1% formaldehyde for 8 min at room temperature and quenched with 1 ml of 2 M glycine for 5 min at room temperature. The cross‐linked cells were washed three times with 20 ml of 0.7 M NaCl.

steps.title: Protoplast formation, nuclei capture with ConA‐beads,...
steps.instruction: Protoplast formation, nuclei capture with ConA‐beads, antibody incubation, and pA/G‐Tn5 transposome assembly followed the histone modification protocol. After fragmentation, crosslinks were reversed by incubating overnight at 65°C with 3 μl of 5 M NaCl. RNA was removed by RNase A treatment (37°C for 30 min), proteins were removed by Proteinase K (53°C for 1 h), and proteases were removed by inhibitors (room temperature for 5 min). Libraries were amplified by PCR as described.

59
 to remove adapters and low‐quality sequences. The cleaned reads were then aligned to the spike‐in sequences using BWA (0.7.

61
 was used to count the number of successfully mapped reads. The sample with the fewest mapped reads was selected as the baseline for spike‐in normalization. The normalized data were then re‐aligned to the reference genome using BWA (0.7.18), with reads having a quality score below 20 being filtered out using samtools (1.21). The alignment results were then processed using Picard (Picard Tools, developed by the Broad Institute) for sorting and deduplication. Peak calling was performed using MACS2 (2.2.9.

62
, with IgG controls used to correct for background. The “‐B” flag was used to generate histone modifications and control lambda.bdg files, which were subsequently compared using the bdgcmp function in MACS

steps.title: ChIPQC (1
steps.instruction: ChIPQC (1.42.0, Bioconductor‐ChIPQC) was applied for quality control of the fCUT&Tag analysis. Spark.py (2.6.

63
 was used to generate a genomic browser view, incorporating the ‐gs parameter to ensure that all groups are autoscaled to a unified y‐axis. Peak annotation was performed using ChIPseeker (1.42.

64
 to assign genomic features to the identified peaks. Correlation analysis was carried out using the multiBigwigSummary and plotCorrelation functions of deepTools (3.3.

65
, while heatmaps were generated using the computeMatrix and plotHeatmap functions of deepTools (3.3.5).

description: Schematic overview of the fCUT&Tag‐Seq workflow for filamentous fungi. Illustration of the key steps in the optimized fCUT&Tag‐Seq protocol in filamentous fungi is shown, covering key steps from protoplast preparation to sequencing. The method involves digitonin permeabilization to enhance nuclear accessibility. fCUT&Tag‐Seq, fungal Cleavage Under Targets and Tagmentation‐Sequencing. Schematic overview of the optimized fCUT&Tag‐Seq method for detecting chromatin‐binding proteins. The workflow diagram illustrates the key steps of the fCUT&Tag‐Seq protocol for analyzing chromatin‐binding proteins in fungi, including formaldehyde crosslinking, protoplast preparation, nuclear extraction, and subsequent fCUT&Tag‐Seq procedures.
